speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three with Irving Eisner. Mr. Eisner, you were telling us about this rabbi who asked you to help him cut his beard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a custom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What-- how did this come about? Tell us. No, because he noticed, and we knew already, that the more you are religious or-- or your appearance is Jewish, like a beard, the more sufferings you probably are going to endure. So he knew time is going-- time is coming to go to-- to the camps. He didn't want to go with a beard. And we heard what happened, how-- how the Germans [HEBREW] not-- not shearing the beard, not-- only pulling pieces and the-- the the-- the flesh together. So how terrible torturing they went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked like he was smart enough not to go like this. And he didn't go with my transport. It was the first, second, and third. I went with the second. So even so, though he was in my ghetto, but he was considered a higher-- he went with the committee people, who were allowed by the Germans to be out of the city. Just for the sleeping you came into the camp. So he went with the last transport. And he survived. By coincidence, I met with him in New York. He left, but he died still young. He died young. And I met him. Would you remember at all before the war, what these rabbis-- what the views were, the opinions were, about the fate of the Jews in Europe? What did they have to say about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- I only can speak about my own rabbi and my-- to whom my father belonged. My father never belonged to a special-- like, you know, they were Hasidic. They belonged to one certain rabbi. They used to go for Yom Tov there, and they used to go for special meetings. My father believed in everyone. He was Orthodox, but still, because this rabbi that I mentioned before, [? Oliver ?] Shalom Aharon Teitelbaum-- he was a rabbi of several villages, so we belonged to him. For example, he used to come every summer to our village to take a bath, you know, this mineral waters, you know. He used to stay in our place, in my place, in our father's place. We had a big house, and we gave him a room. So my father would go to him for-- for everything. But their view was they were waiting for mashiach as we wait today [? of ?] a more power. [HEBREW] faith. No matter what happened to us, they believed hakol l'tovah It's for good. It's a hashgacha. It is God who wants it that way-- that way-- and we have to take it, no matter what. And we didn't dare to go against the rabbi's wish. We didn't-- my father wouldn't go against. Even so, he understood differently. He came to this rabbi. He said to him, I have some money. I want to go away. I can buy tickets for myself, my family. This rabbi told him, Mr.-- [INAUDIBLE] [GERMAN] It does not-- it-- it [? doesn't ?] consider us. It will never come to us. It would not reach us. That was it, and my father didn't move. So I don't-- I'm not so advanced in-- in knowledge to know what really was behind. But they believed [HEBREW]. What will happen will happen. We have to stay where we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did happen from the ghetto in Chust in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto in Chust, we were taken to a brick factory. And there slowly, slowly they-- we went through-- through checking, and they took away whatever we could from us-- they could. And we were taken. We were walking another two kilometers to the train. And by checking us again were those guys who were really nothing with nothing. All of a sudden they became big shots.

speaker: interviewer
text: The locals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, the locals. I had a pair, for example, of new leather shoes. My father made for all of us, made to measure by a-- by a-- a shoemaker. And they looked on my shoes. They took away my shoes, my brother's shoes. They gave me a pair of old, torn shoes. And everybody had to give them still some gold or silver from the pocket. We were kind of lucky because we did not live in Chust long, so they-- they didn't know us, that we have money. But nevertheless, I remember after hiding all the jewelry which I could-- they never found it, that's a different story-- I took in my pocket my-- my father's gold watch, the pocket watch with a long chain. They used to-- he got it-- he got it for his wedding. And I took a ring and 50-- a $50 bill. I remember we had some dollars. I have to give them something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to the check-up, I said no. They don't know me. I have nothing. I took out of my pocket a few so-called pengo, Hungarian money. Emptied out my pockets. I said, this is for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the watch and the ring I took with me. The ring didn't arrive with me. I don't know what happened to the way. I gave it to my sister or something. But that watch, I came-- brought it with me to Birkenau. And that was my welcome to Birkenau. But I think you want to ask me later on about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to give them. They emptied out our pockets, and they made us march to the train. The train was waiting. There were those cattle wagons, you know, those boxcars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody, about 80 people, were just pushed into one wagon. And they closed the door, and they filled up the train, and we start moving. And the Hungarians took us, naturally, till a place called Kosice, which is across-- beginning of Czechoslovakia. Kosice, Kaschau. There the Hungarians gave over the train to the Germans. They put some water. They opened up the doors. They put water in. And the Germans took over the train. The Germans were sitting in the back on one of-- last wagon. And we began to look through the window, because of one little window was there, with wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, what direction the train is going to go. Are we going to Hungary, as we were told, or are we going somewhere else? And people who were older than I am, taller than I, that you-- begin to look. The train is moving, moving, moving, moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, they notice it's going to the hills, going to Poland. Then already we became a little bit depressed. Poland is not good. And that's how we went to Poland. The first night we traveled, we still had food. The second-- in the wagon, I remember everybody took a corner where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with your mother and your brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: And sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we took one corner. And that's the way we settled down. This is our corner. And my mother still had food with her. And later on, as time passed by, it became worse because it became warm. People started to yell. People started to be cranky. I don't recall how they used a washroom. Whatever they used I can't recall now. But I think there was a corner somewhere and pails, I think. But I'm not sure of that. I do not remember if I went, or they went. I can't remember this. It was panic. And we went like this two nights, two days, until we arrived. We arrived-- the first time I saw people working in different clothing, with striped jackets and pants, it was in Krakow. I saw the sign. We knew we're in Poland. And from Krakow, the train began to move slowly, slowly, and we didn't know where we are going [? till we ?] arrived to Brzezinka or called Birkenau, still not knowing where we are going. And that's how we arrived to-- from the ghetto to the camp. It was the first day of Shavuot, 1944. I don't remember the English date. It's a general date. But it was Shavuot, 1944. A shabbat-- a shabbat-- we arrived to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first sight of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sight was-- they blinded our eyes. When they opened the doors, it was a ramp. And we started to take-- they told us, leave everything inside, and it-- it's going to be given to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took as much as we could-- some clothing-- instead of one jacket, we had two jackets, you know, and [INAUDIBLE] We were starting to walk all together. My mother, I, my younger sister, and another younger sister, a brother, and a twin, one of the twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked straight with everybody else. On the sides were standing Jewish kapos. Jewish first of all, workers with the stripes. Later we found out that there were some civilians. They were kapos, among them Jewish too. They had to do their job. We were told not to take nothing, and we start going and going and going. And we also saw a part in Birkenau, like a camp, where old people, like bubbes and zaydes, old, grey people are walking with their children, swinging them, walking with them. So I said to my mother, it looks like that we are going to work, the young people. And you people with the babies are going to stay in the camp. And once a week, Shabbas, or Sunday we're going to meet. It was a picture of life. And we start walking. And as we walked, my mother somehow-- I don't know how she has a feeling there's not good here. And she told us kinderlach, [YIDDISH]. Children, hold your hands together. Let's not separate. So we had a whole line like this, five of us. And we started walking, walking, walking with a baby. And when we arrived, there was standing Mengele. Later I found out that his name was Mengele. And white gloves. A little something in his hand. And he said, looked at the faces, and he said, right, left, right, left. I don't know what it-- rechts, links, rechts, links. Nobody knew what it means. And somehow my mother told him. I mentioned it before. [GERMAN]. I'm 39 years old. Ich [GERMAN] arbeiten. I want to work, I will work. Let me please live. From where she got this idea, till today I don't know. But he cut her off. I took my small brother under my coat, and I smuggled him in to the-- to the other side, not to the death. My mother, my little sister, and 10-year-old sister were taken right away to the gas chambers. I didn't know, I found out only next day what's going on. We found out before, but what happened to my mother, I didn't know until next morning. And so we started to go. There was another gate, another selection. I just showed my pictures. Anyway, we marched. They brought us to a place. It was nice and warm. [INAUDIBLE] Shavuot, Friday afternoon. Number one, they cut our hair completely. I had big, curly hair. In the middle, they left a little stripe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, they have those machines, you know? Cutters. Zero, one and two, numbers. So zero was completely closer to your scalp. One a little bit higher, two was higher. So they cut us with number two. Little bit hair was left, but in the middle, they cut it with a zero, to have a stripe about two inches wide to be recognized. In case something happen, we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from there, they took us to showers, to showers, play showers. Regular waters. And we went to the showers, I and my brother, the younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you tattooed at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --On your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We went to the showers. And after showers, we had to leave our civilian clothes that we brought from home. So they gave us, like, the striped ones. Thin like pajamas, like paper, you know, thin. A jacket and a pants. No underwear, nothing. Just a jacket and a cap. A mutze, it was called, a mutze. And I was holding my chain still. I didn't know where to put it. There were no pockets in those jackets. And I came out from the washroom-- from the showers, excuse me. And a piece of the chain was hanging out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the kapos happens to be a Jewish boy. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, from Poland. He says to me, [NON-ENGLISH] What do you have in your hand? I say it's a watch, it's my father's watch. He says to me, give it to me. [NON-ENGLISH]. I said why? It's my father's. I want to have it. So he took his clobber stick, you know those like police have, rubber sticks. And he hit me on my back. I fell to the cement. My brother and some people helped me out-- up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says again, give it to me. I said, no, no, it's my father's. It's-- I can't give it away. He clobbered me twice, I fell again. This time I couldn't get up so quickly. Still people start to yell, give him, give him, give him. I said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he tells me a second time-- he shows me over there. You see over there that building? I said, so what? I see buildings. You see that chimney over there? I said, so what? A chimney's a chimney, so what? What comes out of a chimney? I said, smoke, smoke and some flames. I say, so what? I still don't understand. So he says to me, you are going from here to that place. You will come out through the smoke. Your life will be turned into smoke and to fire. So what do you need a watch for? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what he's talking. Nobody knew. And he said to me again, you are going to be burned. Give me the watch! And he spoke in Yiddish all the time. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I didn't know what to do. I took the watch. I threw it, all my power, to the cement. It broke into pieces. He clobbered me again a few times. I was laying on the floor for a few minutes. And he took a handkerchief and picked up the pieces and left. Then we started to walk-- it was already late afternoon-- to the barracks, you know, To the barracks. And we start to talk. What's going on here? And we start to feel some smell. Smell? So people start to talk. One says it's garbage, once says it's cleaning from-- from the stables, you know, the-- whatever. But there were some doctors amongst us, physicians. medical, you know, physicians. They said, it smells like human flesh. We thought, look each other. What [INAUDIBLE] talk? Human flesh? And we went to the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at night already, took a few hours. We found out that they are gassing the people and burning them, and the smell and the smoke and the flames are from people. Then I knew already. And they told us. We found out also. Went to the left, went to death, and those who went to the right, other way around. I don't remember what sides we went. So I found out that my mother was burned that night. So was my little sister and my baby sister. My brother was with me the first three days in Birkenau. He survived Birkenau till 1944, till Sukkot, which is in September. He was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I know this? Because him and his cousin, a year younger-- he's alive in Haifa. They were together. They were runners, you know, to-- to deliver some food, mail. They were spared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they were taken to the gas chambers three times. Twice, he went in under the-- in the washroom in the dirt under the boards, him and the cousin. And at night they went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The third time, my-- my brother said he doesn't want to hide anymore. He wants to be killed. He says, what's he going to go home? To whom? He didn't know that somebody is alive, and he was killed. Shavuot-- I mean, Simchat Torah, 1944. So then we found out really what's going on here. As a first experience, I saw that people was-- when I went to the latrine, out to the washroom-- I saw in the corner some bodies with caulk, with white stuff, covered. You know, they painted them for the disinfection, for the smell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I saw my first time dead bodies around us. And we found out what they're-- what they're are doing. So then I knew that I'm going to be next. I was skinny, very skinny. I was short.

speaker: interviewer
text: What am I going to do? They will take me here, because they came to make selection not just by the gate, by the entrance. They came every morning or every second morning to the barracks to look again for weak people to be taken out. So I knew they were going to take me another day, another what-- what--.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the second day or the second day, they came to look for tradespeople, people with trades. Mainly carpenters, they wanted, and painters, and blacksmiths-- schlossers in Yiddish and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I have? I have nothing. I came from the school, and I-- I have no trade, no profession. [NON-ENGLISH] What shall I do? I knew if I stay here, it's finish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, I saw people the age of my father from my second village who were also in lumber business with my father. They were 42, 43 years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden I see they're stepping aside between the carpenters. I say, carpenters? [LAUGHS] You are carpenters? You never put your hand to cold water. You are business-- you are carpenter? I can be a carpenter too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went over there, and I took my brother with me. But then the Germans came. They were  there, actually, and started to count. And what happened was, because my father was shorter than I and younger four years, they took him to the other side. And they took me away as a carpenter to Warsaw, where the ghetto was. 1943 is the uprise ghetto. And I never worked as a carpenter. I cleaned the ghetto. But that was how by miracle, I left Birkenau after three nights sleeping there. That's how we got out of Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we leave Auschwitz and you tell us about how you got to Warsaw, were there many Jewish kapos in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I went to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in Auschwitz. Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish kapos in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only saw Jewish, Jews. They say it was a camp for non-Jews. But I didn't see. I was-- I was-- around me were only Jews. All the camp what I saw. I don't remember today in what camp I-- what number barrack I was. But there were only Jews around us. I didn't know about some other people living-- being in prisons here. I heard there were some others too, like the Russians and so on. I didn't see them. I was among hundreds and hundreds and hundreds, among thousands and thousands of Jews, only Jews, Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with any Hungarian Jews, or were you mixed up with other Jews as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- mostly our transport went together. Hungarians, Hungarians. But next block or so were all kinds of Jews. We didn't have time for those things, even to-- to-- to mingle or something. Everybody was looking for a way to stay alive. Another day, another-- another hour-- and how to-- how to just continue living. Never mind to survive, to be alive after several months. But for the moment, to be alive, to do something. In many cases, you had to decide in a second what to do, what to do. Go this way, go that way, stay here. Sometimes it worked, sometimes not.

speaker: interviewer
text: With me, I believe, I don't know how, that there was-- somebody dictated me what to do. I did the right steps. It just so happened. I know many tried also, and they didn't work. Didn't work. How can you tell? And the Jewish kapos in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau, nobody stayed alive. Birkenau-- you see, Auschwitz had three camps. Camp number one was called Auschwitz, where the sign is Arbeit Macht Frei. Second camp was Birkenau. Birkenau was strictly Birkenau death camp. Killing people. There was no selections there for-- for life. Or you stayed one or two days till they were selected. If not, they-- there was killing there. The next camp was Buna. Or they have there a rubber factory, rubber factory. Many people went to work. It was called Mein Haus or something. That was called Auschwitz number three. Birkenau was in the middle. That was extermination camp, strictly extermination camp. From Auschwitz, people who couldn't work already were marched to Birkenau to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, you leaving Auschwitz? Your mother and sisters have been exterminated. You're leaving your brother behind. You're a religious man. What were your thoughts about God and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Let me put it this way. If you call it life, there was no life. There wasn't called life. In Birkenau was much, much easier to die than to stay alive. I had with me some thoughts. There was no one to talk to. There was no one to turn to. And I was-- I didn't even think about my parents not there or my sisters or brothers. I was involved somehow with myself. How to-- how to breathe, how to stay alive. Never mind what's going on around me. Everybody-- I have no father with me, no sister, nothing. I was alone, and I had to fight to stay-- I wanted to live as long as I can. Mind you, there were cases later on in the labor camps where I asked God like this. When the Americans used to come to bombard Munchen, Munich, and I was beside Munich, I beg God, please, throw down one bomb on us. I don't want to live anymore because I have so much pain. That's later on, I will tell about maybe. But it didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know who was around me, who was with me. I fought to stay alive. And I-- I-- I once-- and one night, when everybody was asleep, somehow I turned to God. Nobody wants to listen. So I would think to myself, maybe he's listening. I had emunah. So is there a God, or isn't it? I-- I-- I couldn't decide. But I'm trying. Maybe he does, maybe he does not listen. Do I know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the back of the barrack. Everybody-- I don't remember what camp it was-- maybe Birkenau or one of the labor camps-- I don't recall. Everybody was asleep. There was a door in the front and a little window in the back. We were 80 people sleeping in this barrack. And I went to the-- everybody was asleep. And I raised my hand to God. And I told him, dear God, I would like to have a little conversation with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as if he said, let me-- what do you want? I said, I'll tell you what I want. I said to him, listen, dear God, I always have faith in you. And I wanted to let you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see what they're doing here. They're taking little kids from the arms of their parents, they're taking old people, and they're throwing them into the fires.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know at that time that American Jews are still alive. I was thinking that is the end of everyone. So I said to God, so what do you want? He's asking me. I said, I tell you. If it goes on like this, you will be bankrupt, dear God. You will be-- you will have to close down your business. So he says to me, what you mean? I said, who is going to pray to you? I said, your-- the kids are getting killed. So who's going to survive? Who's going to-- to mention your name? Ivan and Petrov and so on? [INAUDIBLE] Patricia, and who's going to pray to you? So he says, so you-- what do you want? I said, I make a deal. Even so, you don't make deals with God. But I'm trying to make a deal. If I will come out of this hellish fires, excuse for my expression [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: I will offer my life to bring back Jewish kids to you because many of them will turn away from you. But I will try to win them back. As long as I am able to live, I will teach them, I will educate them. OK, he says to me, go to sleep, go to sleep. I went to sleep. And today I am here. In 1946 I started to teach at the DP camps in Germany, and I am still teaching today. My-- this is my last official year. He kept his promise. I certainly kept my promise. And as long as I can, my brain serves me, my strength gives me, I will continue, because I learned, and I have in my mind-- I had in my mind King David when he was in troubles. He saw death around him. He said one day, [HEBREW]. I will not die. I will live. But living just like this doesn't mean anything. [HEBREW]. The reason because I should be able to tell. And I believe one of the reasons that I can be in this interview right now is because [HEBREW]. I am alive, and I want to tell the truth. Not just stories-- the facts, everyday facts that people should know. And Psalms 118 again, [HEBREW], which means, I'm doing this generations to know. Children who are going to be born should tell for their children what has happened. [HEBREW] have tortured me. They made me pain. [HEBREW]. For some reason, which is not for me to-- to know how, he didn't let me die. And that's why I'm doing this interview, and I'm going to do it as long as I can. [HEBREW]. The children must know. Otherwise, it can happen again, to my opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hold that belief, that it can happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I strongly believe. Tell you why I believe. It might not happen in the same tragic way. But I grew up in Czechoslovakia, and we were very, very lovely democratic country. We had our synagogues. We had our Hebrew schools. We are free to practice whatever in the world you want. Yet, yet, three years took it, from 1940 to 1944. Look what happened. Look what happened. You don't need-- you need to want to meshuggeneh. They were talking. They were keep on talking and talking and talking until you believe it. That's what Hitler did, [HEBREW]. And it might happen again. It might not happen so drastically. That's why our duty, your duty, and my duty is to tell the young generation. And thanks to Spielberg, that only if they will know, they-- they will try to prevent it. There's still time to prevent and not let it come to a stage that nothing you can be done, because it might happen again.
